crime.value: Murder of Andre at Aversa - thrown from balcony
crime.quote: the voice of Andre asking mercy from his murderers

victim.value: Andre - Joan's husband, about to be crowned, making him a threat to conspirators
victim.quote: Andre, mounted on one of the most fiery of all the steeds he had tamed, galloped beside his wife

closed_world.value: The royal court and convent of St. Peter's at Majella in Aversa during the hunting expedition
closed_world.quote: the convent of St. Peter's at Majella was converted into a royal residence

culprit_and_motive.value: Joan and accomplices including Robert of Cabane and Bertrand of Artois - political conspiracy
culprit_and_motive.quote: the moments of madness passed like a dream in the arms of Bertrand of Artois

resolution.value: Joan is eventually executed by Hungarian barons - justice through retribution
resolution.quote: This was the third corpse that was thrown over the balcony at Aversa.

suspect_architecture.value: Multiple conspirators in Andre's murder including Joan (queen/wife with motive of freedom from unwanted marriage), Robert of Cabane, Bertrand of Artois (lover), and various accomplices who were later tortured
suspect_architecture.quote: the queen saw before her eyes the carts of infamy and the torture of her accomplices

red_herring.value: The hunting expedition to Aversa presented as innocent royal entertainment, masking the planned assassination location
red_herring.quote: Andre's intention was to spend several days hunting between Capua and Aversa

social_world.value: Neapolitan royal court with complex hierarchies - crown officers, courtiers, nobles, religious figures like Friar Robert, and the power dynamics between Hungarian and Angevin claims to the throne
social_world.quote: officers of the crown were assembled regularly twice a day, and persons of importance

alibi.value: Charles claims he cannot attend the hunt because his wife is unwell, offering a falcon instead while giving the notary a 'petrifying glance'
alibi.quote: it will be impossible for me to go to-morrow, for my wife is very unwell

reveal_mechanic.value: Joan's final recognition and acceptance of divine justice when shown the silk and gold cord - the murder weapon that connects all three deaths at Aversa
reveal_mechanic.quote: one of the men showed her a cord of silk and gold

media_and_audience.value: The ringing of three hundred church bells announcing the king's death, demonstrating how royal events are publicly communicated and mourned throughout Naples
media_and_audience.quote: they were suddenly awakened by the bells of the three hundred churches

_meta.title: ebook alexandre dumas pere joan of naples
